build a kanban board to track the teams tasks

summary.text:
**Defining the Scope**

I'm focused on strictly interpreting the user's request. My priority is to "build a kanban board to track the team's tasks." I'm adhering to a philosophy of radical simplicity, striving for a clean, intuitive, and minimal design. I am not trying to make any design changes.

**Implementing the Kanban Board**

I've determined we need a three-column Kanban board – To Do, In Progress, and Done. The design will be modern, clean, and simple with an emphasis on typography and spacing. No mock data; instead, I'll leverage the `/api/sync` endpoints for real-time multiplayer synchronization, including short-polling. I'll utilize `window.__sandboxEnvId` and incorporate real workspace team members and tasks.

**Prioritizing User Data** I'm now focusing on incorporating user data, specifically avatar URLs, into the Kanban board. I'll be using standard `` tags for displaying these avatars. I am now completely focused on delivering the completed application code immediately, following the instruction to output code directly in markdown blocks and avoid local file system operations. I am prioritizing speed and efficiency as well.

**Prioritizing Code Output**

I'm now laser-focused on immediate code output. The instruction is clear: no `write_file`, just markdown code blocks directly in chat. I've double-checked for any caveats, but the command is explicit. Now I need to build, then output the `index.html` block at minimum for interactive web apps, as per the final instruction. Project Ollie looks to have a slightly more complex status system than just To Do, In Progress, and Done.

**Refining the Design**

I've decided to refine the Kanban board's status columns to include `IN_PROGRESS`, `PENDING_REVIEW`, `COMPLETED`, and `CANCELLED`. I'm committed to a minimalist and beautiful design with a single self-contained `index.html` file. The design system will be either Polaris or Material Design 3 (M3). I'm checking specific design rules, including CSS layout variables and M3 tokens, like primary color values.

**Defining the Aesthetics**

I'm now fully immersed in the aesthetic details. I'm focusing on the typography; I've decided on Google Sans for headers (16px, 500 weight) and Google Sans Text for body and card text (13-14px, 400 weight). The design calls for flat backgrounds, clean rounded borders, subtle outlines, and generous padding for cards and columns. I'm committed to simplicity, avoiding unnecessary embellishments. I'll populate the board with tasks assigned to Ajay Yadav, Belinda Preno, and Daniel Giuditta, with their avatar URLs.

**Constructing the Output**

I'm now zeroed in on generating the `index.html` file directly within the chat. I've decided to use standard CSS or simple utility-like CSS for safety and cleanliness, maintaining a Polaris-styled design. I'll include the necessary `<script>` tag for the Tailwind CSS CDN. My focus is on delivering a complete and robust Kanban board that looks beautiful and is strictly minimalist.

**Structuring the Layout**

I'm now deep in defining the HTML structure. I'm focusing on a clean, minimal layout with a top bar featuring the workspace title and status. The main area will be a grid with three columns and simple, styled column headers. I am prioritizing a flat design with rounded borders and outlines. Within the columns, I'll incorporate task cards with Google Sans typography, assignee avatars, and action buttons.

**Adding Interactive Elements**

I'm now integrating drag-and-drop functionality for the task cards, with arrow buttons as an alternative. These actions will sync with the `/api/sync` endpoint. I'm adding an "Add Task" button with a modal/form for creating new tasks, including title, description, and assignee selection from team members.

I'm starting to generate the initial tasks, according to project ollie, including assignee and status. This helps validate the overall design.

**Refining Task Interaction**

I'm now fully immersed in the interactive elements. I am adding the drag-and-drop features, starting with defining all of the event handlers. I'm focusing on the "Add Task" button implementation, using an inline form instead of a modal to keep the minimalist design. I am also implementing smooth CSS animations. The syncing logic is being continuously tested.

**Defining Task Interaction**

I'm deep in defining task interaction now. I've expanded the "Add Task" feature with an inline form for title, description, and assignee selection from real team members. Upon saving, it adds the task, syncs the state, resets the form, and re-renders the board. I'm adding a minimal trash icon for deleting tasks.

**Optimizing Data Synchronization**

I'm now implementing robust data syncing, focused on preventing UI flickering during collaborative editing. I'm separating local form state (task addition) from the synchronized task data, which allows for robust non-interfering synchronizations. I am also implementing a better data loader and saver, and am also implementing the logic for checking if textareas are being edited.

sandboxFiles.name: [NTK] Project Ollie Eng Design
[UX Research plan] Project Ollie: Baseline Workflow & Agent Delegation
sandboxFiles.content:
Core Objective
To map the data trails, tool ecosystems, and context-sharing habits of knowledge workers in the real world, as well as their desires and expectations regarding AI delegation.
Participants
* Location & Availability: Must currently live in the U.S. and be able to attend a 90-minute, in-person study session at the NYC Google Office between July 6th and July 24th with their own laptop.1
* Company Profile: Employed at a private educational institute or a commercial business/organization (excluding public education, nonprofits, and government).1
* Software & Tool Usage:
   * Their company must use Google Workspace (Gmail, Drive, Docs, or Calendar) with official work email addresses.1
   * The "actual work" they do must involve co-editing shared documents and spreadsheets (such as Google Docs or Sheets). Users who primarily manage tasks in dedicated project management tools (like Jira, Asana, or Trello) are disqualified.1
* Role & Authority: Must have a role in their business's decision to purchase digital tools and software (either as a sole decision-maker, part of a collective group, or as an influencer).1
* Collaboration Level: They must spend at least half or more of their work week collaborating with others on projects, tasks, or shared goals.1
Details here: Ollie current needs and painpoints 


Research Questions 
* RQ1 (The Fragmented Truth): What does a knowledge worker's true "to-do list" actually look like across formal tools (Jira, Asana) and informal channels (Slack stars, open browser tabs, mental notes)?
* RQ2 (Context Breadcrumbs): When a user moves a task forward, what specific artifacts (links, documents, specific chat sentences) represent the "proof of work" that Ollie will eventually need to ingest?
* RQ3 (The Translation Gap): How do users naturally summarize a complex day of work into a project update? What details do they filter out, and what do they consider critical?
* RQ4 (Agent Delegation Triggers): What tasks do users actually want to hand off versus what they want to keep control over? What parameters (triggers, boundaries, outputs) define a successful Sojo Agent assignment?
Session script
Artifact & Task Audit (15 mins)
* Goal: Uncover the hidden, informal task ecosystems.
* Activity: Have them open their laptop and show you their current "active stack."
* Prompts to use:
   * "Show me the 3 things you know you have to do today but aren't written down in a formal project board."
   * "Look at your open browser tabs or flagged emails right now—what tasks do those represent?"
   * "Where is the conversation happening about your top priority right now? Can you show me that Slack thread/email chain?"
The "Human Ollie" Walkthrough (25 mins)
* Goal: Treat the user's memory and screen as the database to see what Ollie should have done.
* Activity: Walk backward through their last 24–48 hours of work. Have them literally point at things on their screen.
* Prompts to use:
   * "You opened this Google Doc at 10 AM and left a comment. If Ollie was a smart assistant watching this, what is the 'core project update' it should extract from that action?"
   * "If an AI wrote an update based purely on this Slack thread you just showed me, what would it miss? What's the unwritten context here?"
   * "Look at this completed task. What were all the scattered pieces of data (spreadsheets, links, client feedback) that were required to get it done?"
AI Agents tasks (15 mins)
* Goal: Use their real data to define the scope and boundaries of the agents.
* Activity: Review the real tasks identified in Phase 1 and 2. Print out blank "Sojo Agent Job Description" cards (or use a digital whiteboard) and fill them out together.
* Prompts to use:
   * "Look at this specific task you spent an hour on yesterday. If an AI Agent could have done it, what is the exact trigger to start it? (e.g., 'When a new CSV lands in this folder' or 'When a client pings me via email')."
   * "What does the agent need access to on your laptop to successfully do this?"
   * "What is the exact output you expect? A draft email? A spreadsheet update? And where should it put it?"
